description: You are working as a data-science engineer who must “headlessly” scrape a collection of local HTML pages, consolidate the data, remove duplicates and produce both a cleaned CSV file and a short processing log. Context already present in the container: 1. Three static HTML pages are located in /home/user/website/pages/ ├── page1.html ├── page2.html └── page3.html 2. Every file contains one table with the exact attribute id="weather". • The table header is always the three columns: “Date”, “Temperature”, “Humidity”. • All body rows look like: YYYY-MM-DD#### Task you must carry out from the Linux terminal (do NOT modify the existing HTML): A. Write and execute a headless scraping program or script of your choice (Python, Node.js … your call) that 1. Reads all *.html files inside /home/user/website/pages/ (discover them programmatically, do not hard-code the filenames). 2. Extracts every row of the table id="weather". 3. Builds an in-memory dataset with the columns exactly named: date,temperature_c,humidity_percent – Keep the order shown above. 4. Drops perfectly identical rows that might appear in more than one source file. 5. Sorts the final set strictly in ascending order by the “date” column. B. Persist two output artefacts: 1. CSV file Path: /home/user/output/weather_clean.csv Encoding: UTF-8, Unix line endings, and NO extra blank line at the end. Exact format (first line is the header): date,temperature_c,humidity_percent YYYY-MM-DD,##,## … one line per unique row, sorted as specified. 2. Log file Path: /home/user/output/scrape.log Encoding: UTF-8, Unix line endings. Must contain, in the exact order processed, one line per HTML file that was successfully parsed plus a final summary line. Line format (including the leading “[OK]” tag and a single space after it): [OK] /absolute/path/to/file.html After the last “[OK] …” line, write: Total unique rows written: N (where N is the integer count of the unique rows that ended up in weather_clean.csv). C. Verification hints you MUST satisfy (automated tests will check): • The directory /home/user/output/ must exist and be writable by the current user. • The CSV header must be present and spell-checked exactly. • Dates must keep the original YYYY-MM-DD format from the HTML. • The numeric columns must not contain units, only the raw integers found in the table cells. • Logging lines must start with “[OK] ” (square brackets, capital OK, trailing space). • The summary line must be the very last line of scrape.log. • No other files should be created inside /home/user/output/. Produce whatever intermediate scripts, virtual-environment folders or helper files you need, but only the two deliverables in /home/user/output/ will be graded. Work step by step, use shell commands, show your work as you go. Good luck!
